lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms delivering bespoke human personal services to external customers, including personal fitness training, wedding and party planning, personal organizing and shopping, concierge and genealogy services, personal chefs, doulas, house sitting, porter services, and similar appointment- or project-based personal work.
lens.excluded_activities: Bail bonding and identity-theft protection because their core economics are financial risk or subscription protection; online dating and other software-led platforms; coin- or card-operated machine and locker concessions; social escort, psychic, and fortune-telling services because their risk and delivery models are not comparable; captive providers; and firms outside the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Included firms sell hourly sessions, appointments, retainers, packages, or projects directly to individuals and households. Revenue depends on practitioner time, personalized advice or coordination, trust, scheduling, customer acquisition, and reliable delivery; some categories add in-person coaching, safety, cooking, sitting, or event execution.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS residual mixes regulated surety, digital subscriptions and marketplaces, unattended concessions, intimate or mystical services, and bespoke human coaching and coordination. A single workflow and billing screen would be incoherent, so the lens retains the broad human-delivered appointment and project service cluster and excludes the structurally different models; the narrowing is for construct coherence, not attractiveness.

a.rationale: Current not-yet-automated exposure includes lead response, intake, scheduling, reminders, plan and proposal drafting, vendor research, purchasing support, marketing, documentation, follow-up, and administration. BLS task descriptions show that fitness and event work also requires live demonstration, safety monitoring, client discovery, negotiation, venue inspection, logistics, and deadline execution, which remain human and often physical.
a.change_evidence: Revenue-weighted occupation and task diaries across the included 812990 subactivities, with practitioner, sales, administrative, and delivery hours separated and current tool use identified, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No source measures the wage-weighted task mix of the narrowed six-digit population; fitness and event-planning occupations cover workers and employers outside 812990.

rho.rationale: Scheduling, intake, marketing, research, drafting, and routine follow-up can be implemented with accessible software over five years. Adoption is limited by fragmented microbusiness systems, privacy and sensitive personal data, low process volume, practitioner resistance, client expectations of personal attention, safety obligations, and the cost of errors in events or physical coaching.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after labor hours, conversion, retention, service quality, incident rates, and practitioner capacity from AI deployments at multi-location training, planning, concierge, organizer, chef, doula, or sitting firms would establish implementation.
caveats: Implementation economics vary sharply between repeat appointments, one-time events, physical services, and advisory projects.

e.rationale: Census lists many human-delivered personal services that fit the narrowed lens, including fitness training, wedding and party planning, personal organizers and shoppers, concierge, genealogy, doulas, chefs, house sitting, and porter or shoeshine services. The code also contains substantial excluded financial, digital, machine-concession, and other incomparable models, and no revenue-weighted subindustry mix was found.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level 2022 NAICS business-register or Economic Census special tabulation by index activity, external service revenue, independence, and normalized EBITDA would determine the eligible share.
caveats: The eligible share is especially uncertain because 812990 is a residual code and the supplied LMM count uses a common business-services margin across unlike activities.

s5.rationale: Many included firms are practitioner-led and depend on personal reputation, relationships, and delivery, depressing transferability. Multi-practitioner firms with brands, recurring client books, standardized programs, planners or coordinators, and transferable staff can sell, but the share is unknown and likely below less owner-dependent service industries.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year DealStats, BizBuySell, PitchBook, or GF Data extract of verified control transfers for the retained 812990 activities, matched to firm counts and owner-dependence indicators, would replace this estimate.
caveats: No control-sale rate or owner-age series was found for the narrowed cluster; a practitioner retiring without a transferable organization is not a qualifying transfer.

q.rationale: Hourly sessions, packages, retainers, and fixed project fees allow operators to retain near-term productivity gains, particularly when demand exceeds practitioner capacity. Low entry barriers, transparent digital alternatives, easy customer switching, bespoke scope expansion, and pressure to add responsiveness or personalization should pass a substantial share back to clients over five years.
q.change_evidence: Engagement-level price, hours, scope, renewal, discount, and contribution-margin cohorts before and after automation across multiple retained subactivities would reveal the discounted retained share.
caveats: BLS describes work and demand rather than contract pass-through; pricing and renewal behavior likely differs materially by subactivity.

d5.bridge: BLS projects 2024-2034 employment growth of 12% for fitness trainers and instructors and 5% for meeting, convention, and event planners, two visible but broader occupations overlapping the retained lens. Annualizing those ten-year projections and applying roughly five years suggests modest positive demand. Employment can rise or fall differently from constant-quality service quantity, and both occupations include establishments outside 812990; excluding the code's other retained activities makes the net error direction indeterminate, so the range is wide.
d5.rationale: Health and fitness participation, an aging population, and demand for professionally organized personal events support selected activities, while AI self-service, online content, household budget pressure, and substitution toward platforms or do-it-yourself planning constrain growth. The proxy supports a modest-growth base rather than an industry-wide forecast.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year inflation-adjusted revenue and completed-service panel for retained 812990 activities, weighted by their actual revenue shares and paired with customer-frequency data, would replace the occupation proxy.
caveats: Occupation projections are national ten-year employment forecasts, not five-year demand ratios for the narrowed NAICS population.

o.rationale: Clients can self-serve generic plans, research, scheduling, vendor discovery, and routine advice through software. Accountable operators remain important for live exercise technique and safety, client motivation, sensitive personal judgment, venue and vendor verification, deadline execution, cooking, sitting, doula support, and recovery when a bespoke service fails.
o.change_evidence: Customer-journey and substitution research by retained activity, measuring completed service without a human operator and the consequences for safety, execution, satisfaction, and repeat use, would materially refine the share.
caveats: Operator necessity is high for physical and safety-critical services but much lower for research, planning templates, and routine coordination.

narrative.executive_view: The residual code is too heterogeneous for an undifferentiated operating thesis, so the lens retains bespoke human-delivered coaching, planning, coordination, and household personal services. Those firms have substantial information and administrative work, but delivery often rests on practitioner judgment, trust, physical presence, and owner reputation.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can automate intake, scheduling, reminders, marketing, research, proposals, program drafts, vendor comparisons, documentation, and routine follow-up. It is less able to replace live technique correction, motivation, safety, sensitive judgment, venue inspection, negotiation, cooking, sitting, birth support, or event-day accountability.
narrative.value_capture: Capacity-constrained practitioners and fixed-fee projects may retain gains initially. Low barriers, client switching, digital substitutes, and scope expansion should push part of those gains into lower prices, richer service, or more responsiveness.
narrative.firm_availability: Census identifies many eligible human-service activities, but it also places financial, digital, unattended-machine, and other incomparable models in the same residual code. The eligible share and supplied LMM firm count are therefore unusually uncertain, while owner dependence reduces transferability.
narrative.demand_durability: BLS expects fitness and event-planning employment to grow, supporting demand for two retained activities. The code-wide outlook is less certain because household discretionary budgets, digital self-service, demographics, and activity mix can pull in different directions.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The principal risks are lens-mix uncertainty, absent revenue weights, missing task and deal-flow data, practitioner dependence, fragmented microbusiness systems, sensitive client data, liability in physical or deadline-critical services, and a common-margin firm-count estimate applied to unlike models.
narrative.principal_driver: Personal-service firms combine repeat intake and coordination work with growing selected end markets, creating capacity leverage from AI assistance.
narrative.principal_weakness: Residual-code heterogeneity and owner-dependent human delivery make eligibility, transferability, and aggregate economics unusually uncertain.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 812990 All Other Personal Services
sources.supports: Defines the residual industry and lists bail bonding, machine concessions, consumer buying, wedding planning, dating, and personal fitness among examples; index entries also include concierge, genealogy, doulas, house sitting, personal chefs, organizers, shoppers, porters, and other disparate services.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Fitness Trainers and Instructors
sources.supports: Describes live demonstration, technique and safety monitoring, personalized programs, progress tracking, client motivation, sales and marketing tasks; reports 370,100 jobs in 2024 and 12% projected employment growth from 2024 to 2034.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Meeting, Convention, and Event Planners
sources.supports: Describes client discovery, scope and budget planning, bid solicitation, venue inspection, logistics, event monitoring and bill review; explicitly includes weddings and reports 155,800 jobs in 2024 with 5% projected growth from 2024 to 2034.
